preamble: The transformation from the voiceless alveolo-palatal affricate (t͡ɕ) to the voiceless alveolo-palatal fricative (ɕ) is a common phonetic shift known as deaffrication. This process involves the loss of the initial stop component of the affricate, resulting in a simpler fricative sound. It is often driven by a tendency towards articulatory ease, as producing a fricative requires less effort than an affricate.

examples.note: Deaffrication of /t͡ɕ/ to [ɕ] has been documented in the historical development of Vosgien, an Oïl language.
Historically, non-native Russian [t͡ɕ] in loanwords underwent variable deaffrication in word-initial position in Plautdietsch.

In conversational Mandarin, the palatal affricate /t͡ɕ/ frequently undergoes lenition, where the absence of the stop features (closure and transient noise) can result in a voiceless fricative [ɕ]. This is described as a gradient process and a potential change in progress.

sources: Mechanisms of sub-phonemic change in prescriptive bilingualism: The case of Mexican Plautdietsch | Glossa: a journal of general linguistics. (2022).
A phonetic and phonological analysis of palatalization as a trigger of sound change in the development of Vosgien.
AN INVESTIGATION OF AFFRICATE SIMPLIFICATION IN CONVERSATIONAL MANDARIN - International Phonetic Association.
